comments.content:
[IMAGE:0]Midnight is upon us and so it's time to launch a new Magic Monday. Ask me anything about occultism, and with certain exceptions noted below, any question received by midnight Monday Eastern time will get an answer. Please note:  Any question or comment received after that point will not get an answer, and in fact will just be deleted.  If you're in a hurry, or suspect you may be the 341,928th person to ask a question, please check out the very rough version 1.3 of The Magic Monday FAQ here. 

Also: I will not be putting through or answering any more questions about practicing magic around children. I've answered those in simple declarative sentences in the FAQ. If you read the FAQ and don't think your question has been answered, read it again. If that doesn't help, consider remedial reading classes; yes, it really is as simple and straightforward as the FAQ says.  And further:  I've decided that questions about getting goodies from spirits are also permanently off topic here. The point of occultism is to develop your own capacities, not to try to bully or wheedle other beings into doing things for you. I've discussed this in a post on my blog.

[IMAGE:1]The image? I field a lot of questions about my books these days, so I've decided to do little capsule summaries of them here, one per week.  This is my seventy-sixth published book, the second workbook and fifth volume in the Golden Section Fellowship sequence of occult training textbooks. Earth mysteries, for those who aren't familiar with the term, is a catchall label for all the strange things associated with ancient ruins, folk traditions, places where paranormal things happen much more often than usual. The book on the right, John Michell's visionary masterpiece The View Over Atlantis, was my introduction to the field back in the day, as it was for so many other people -- it didn't hurt that the cover art was by Roger Dean, more famous for all that hallucinatory Yes album cover art -- and this book is partly my homage to Michell and a vanished era, partly a guide to integrating earth mysteries studies with occult training, and partly -- or mostly -- a hands-on guide to finding the weirdness in the place where you live. You can get a copy here if you're in the United States and here elsewhere; I recommend the hardback if you're doing the course, because you're going to put a year of hard use into the book. 

Buy Me A Coffee

Ko-Fi

I've had several people ask about tipping me for answers here, and though I certainly don't require that I won't turn it down. You can use either of the links above to access my online tip jar; Buymeacoffee is good for small tips, Ko-Fi is better for larger ones. (I used to use PayPal but they developed an allergy to free speech, so I've developed an allergy to them.) If you're interested in political and economic astrology, or simply prefer to use a subscription service to support your favorite authors, you can find my Patreon page here and my SubscribeStar page here. 
 
[IMAGE:2]I've also had quite a few people over the years ask me where they should buy my books, and here's the answer. Bookshop.org is an alternative online bookstore that supports local bookstores and authors, which a certain gargantuan corporation doesn't, and I have a shop there, which you can check out here. Please consider patronizing it if you'd like to purchase any of my books online.

And don't forget to look up your Pangalactic New Age Soul Signature at CosmicOom.com.

With that said, have at it!

***This Magic Monday is now closed, and no further comments will be put through. See you next week!***

comments.title: The Dolmen Arch
comments.content:
I’m still grinding away at The Dolmen Arch, and I need a bit of clarification.
On page 23 of Volume II, do I repeat step six of self-healing three times or just once? The instruction to repeat it three times appears on the other six steps, so… three times or just once?
Oh, I can report an uptick in elderly and energy poor people demanding my attention – usually unpleasantly, since I started the Gwyddon Y Fford chapter. I try to be gracious, but… I have limits. On the upside, dogs want my attention – which is much more fun.
Speaking of dogs – Some while ago, I wondered about the Hounds of Annwn while meditating on Air – I’d love a big, ferocious one for a best pal, of course. A sylph with a sense of humor popped up dressed as a Chinese crested dog in the usual white with red ears livery. As usual, I’m a curious child to be humored – it keeps my humility topped off, so… Anyway, I gave it some thought again when news of rebooted dire wolves crossed my desktop. I’m pretty sure it was the same sylph who popped up again dressed as a great big white wolf with red ears. I thought it would be fun to be able to do that, so the sylph looked at me and sort of dressed me up in the same costume in a layer of reality right over my material form. (I gave it back, btw. I think.) We had a good laugh, but I didn’t know what to do with the wolf suit (yeah, yeah, mischief of one kind and another), nor do I know what to make of the encounter. My staves told me I don’t get to know.
Umm…? Is there anything to see here? Or – Just declare it a wonder?
Many thanks.
Rhydlyd, aka Monochrome Nocturnal Crustacean

* * *

May Ron M’s friend Paul, who passed away on April 13, make his transition through the afterlife process with grace and peace.

Hello JMG and Everybody,
The other night, I had a very vivid dream. I'm wondering if anyone is willing to take a stab at its meaning.
In this dream, I was in Crimea on the Black Sea beach. I was young. In my dream, I "knew" that this was Crimea. I was in an empty hut, which was about the size of a 2-car garage. From the window, I saw men hauling boats using ropes. Suddenly, I saw a gigantic tidal wave. I experienced a moment of intense fear and tried to calculate if I could escape. Then I felt calm and thought, "Oh, that's how I'm gonna die," and then I woke up.
Thank you for MM, JMG.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Well, I don't claim to have the least idea what it means, but I've been working with my dreams for a while now and so have some suggestions you might try. Start by making a list of each element in the dream: Crimea, the beach, the hut, and so on. Take them one at a time and, without pausing, write down whatever associations come to mind in relation to them. Free associate, and see what you come up with. Then see if the free associations, strung together, make any sense to you.

Your mileage may vary, of course, but this often works for me.

I kept dreaming about George Washington and the Battle of Trenton (more than three times), still working on that. Perhaps all your American work like Johnny Appleseed and the like has infected my dreamscape as well.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
The symbolism of a dream is of course deeply personal - but you dropped a hint when you mentioned the role Crimea played for you during your childhood, so I'll give it a try although it's very coarse and most things have already been mentioned:

Crimea is a place of your childhood. The death you thought about in the dream can very well symbolize a passage, for example when the child becomes an adult, or when you face some kind of life changing situation. Initiation, in a way. Water as a symbol for emotions, powerful, being out of your conscious control. The men and the (old) boats - past attempts to navigate the "waters", old strategies to stay afloat. Futile, given the power of the water. As is the shelter you're (hiding?) in.

A few questions that you might ask yourself: What has triggered the dream? Have you recently experienced anything that links to the Crimean summers of your childhood (not necessarily intellectually, but possibly emotionally)? Has anything happened during those summers that is still capable of rising strong emotions? Or that has shaped your personality - the way you react to certain situations - in ways that could be symbolized by boats and shelters?

I don't know if this goes in the right direction - but surely there are other questions you could ask your dream. If you meditate and practice some kind of divination these could be used - I have experienced that both can be very helpful to unpack the meanings of a dream. You can for example ask the oracle "What do I need to know about Crimea/the Sea/ ... in my dream?" and then meditate on the result and go on from there. But take it easy and listen to your intuition - at some point it might be better to be content with what you've got already and not go too far, at least for the moment.

Cheers,
Nachtgurke

replies.content: Thank you, Nachtgurke! 😊

comments.content:
Hello JMG, this is my first time participating in the Magic Mondays. I’ve been reading the Natural Magic book and came up with some questions:
-Would you happen to know any of the elemental correspondences for the carob tree?
-If I wanted to find out the correspondences for some plants that are not in the Natural Magic book, where do I begin?
-What are the properties that make beeswax so useful in magic? Bardon uses it for talismans and even figures for elementaries.
-Why is silk a good magic insulator? Do we know more or less how it works?
-And finally, I’ve heard a lot gem experts saying that nowadays it’s very hard to find rubies and sapphires that haven’t been heat treated to enhance their color, as it’s now the standard procedure at the source. Does this process affect their magic properties in any way?
I hope I’m not abusing your time by asking so many questions. I would appreciate any help I can get. Thank you!

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) Nope.

2) The old Doctrine of Signatures, the system by which the correspondences were assigned, is among the subsystems of Renaissance magic that nobody seems to have revived successfully yet. Intensive meditation on the characteristics of the plant you have in mind, trying to relate it to the elements, planets, and signs, is about all I can suggest.

3-4) Nobody knows. We're in the same position as people dealing with gravity before the time of Isaac Newton -- we know more or less what works, but not how or why it works.

5) Yes, it makes the gems much less magically effective. For this reason I strongly recommend using semiprecious stones with similar energies instead, as these are less often adulterated.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Robert Allen Bartlett has a very handy book The Temper of Herbs that works along these lines from the alchemical point of view to help determine temper and correspondences for different plants not in the lore.

replies.content: Nice! I'm glad to hear this.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) Yes.

2) Intention that is not expressed in action is useless. This is true even, or especially, in magic. If you do a working for some purpose, you need to take some practical step as soon as possible to bring the intention of the working about -- that grounds the working in the material plane and gives the energies you've invoked a way to flow into manifestation.

comments.title: Affirmations

Is it effective to string a couple brief affirmations together, or is it preferable to do each one separately?

For example, "I am productive and kind" vs
"I am productive"
and
"I am kind"
each repeated on their own at different times of day?

replies.title: Re: Affirmations
replies.content: I recommend doing one of these at a time -- and by that I don't mean at different times of the day; I mean that you would be better off focusing on one for a time to the exclusion of all else, and then focusing on the other. Intentionality gains power from unity of focus. If you fling all your strength into becoming kind, let's say, you'll achieve that, and then you can work on becoming more productive; that has better results than trying to do two different things at the same time.

replies.title: Re: Affirmations

comments.title: Essene Woes
comments.content:
Dear JMG,

This is probably a situation I should have looked into more when it occurred a few years ago, but due to circumstances I'm thinking about it now.

When you posted the first of the Modern Order of Essenes teachings, I was finishing up the final grade of the Dolmen Arch course. I did the correspondence course version, so I was preparing my examination while the teachings were going up and would have just received the response from my mentor informing me that I had passed a few days before you offered the first Apprentice Grade attunement, which I received.

Previously, as part of the DA curriculum, I had made a talisman of air for general blessing using the "lesser" enchantment techniques, i.e., those calling on one specific element. This had very evident effects. After that I created a talisman of water for dream recall and was quite impressed at the results--I instantly had near perfect dream recall of multiple vivid dream each night and a couple of spontaneous lucid dreams (something I had attained some years prior after many months of disciplined dream recall work, but lost gradually when my life circumstances changed and I ceased to actually record the dreams I was remembering). This lasted for a month with no additional "work" (no other practices meant to increase dream recall), and then gradually tapered off over the next month. I also tried the "greater" enchantment method, the one calling on the two currents, but only for very broad blessing kinds of things where I can't really judge their effectiveness (though notably the air talisman was for "blessing" and still had very clear effects).

I made a new dream recall talisman using the same method right before receiving an Essene attunement. This time I was planning to resume the dream recall practices I had worked with years ago while the talisman was still having an effect to see if I could use it to boost that process. Unfortunately, due to the years that have passed, I do not recall which attunement--I received both the Apprentice and Healer attunements. This time the talisman worked as before and everything was going well for a few days, right up until the night after the attunement, where it seemed to suddenly lose all effect, abruptly "turning off," unlike the gradual fade out I had observed with the prior talisman.

I did not keep up the "manual" dream recall practices, intending to troubleshoot the talisman first.

Around this time, I was deciding what studies I would pursue post-Dolmen Arch, and decided to work on a couple of things unrelated to practical enchantment that I had previously put off to focus on the Dolmen Arch studies. So I kind of didn't give this as much attention as you might expect from the story so far. My focus was shifting after fulfilling my DA commitment.

I was starting to feel like the Essene work might not be for me. I had not done divination prior to beginning it, but did so now and was strongly warned against continuing the Essene work, so I did not continue to become a Master Healer.

I tried a few other DA type enchantments, and was never again able to see notable results from the lesser enchantment methods, in dream recall or otherwise. I never had any results I could positively identify from the greater enchantment method, before or after the Essene attunement.

I pursued other things for a while, and stopped experimenting with DA methods as I dropped the DA daily practices in favor of other things I wanted to try.

A while after that, I picked up Polytheistic Golden Dawn practice following the guidelines you've posted here. Without intending any disrespect to the DA practices, I have to say that the Golden Dawn work seems to suit me much better. I've been doing those practices consistently and have been very happy with the results. Now I'm at the point where I'd like to experiment with practical enchantment of the elemental talisman variety in that system, which is why I started thinking about the weirdness during that overlap between the tail end of my DA practice and my short Essene career.

1) Do you have any diagnosis for what happened then?

2) Is there anything I should be worried about in trying Golden Dawn style practical enchantment?

3) Should I have ended my connection to the Modern Order of the Essenes more formally, rather than just dropping the practices? Is there anything I should do now, although it's been a few years? (I've considered restarting the MOE work a few times, but have been firmly told no by divination each time, though I've gotten positive indications regarding receiving MOE healing from others.)

replies.title: Re: Essene Woes
replies.content:
1) That's really strange. No, and I've never heard from anyone else who had that experience, either.

2) Not that I know of.

3) There isn't really much you can do. Once you receive an initiation or attunement, you've got it, and it never goes away -- it's a little like losing your virginity, in a sense. Simply accept that that's over, pay attention to what you've learned from the experience, and focus on the fundamentals of your new system.

This might be a good place to say something about the curious relation between the magic squares that appear in the original version of the Abramelin working, on the one hand, and the grids of cross-wiring strength numbers that appear in modern deep network training practice, on the other? The vulnerabilities, and the harms that get through them, can be loosely likened to the harmful consequences that the Abramelin book suggests will befall those who attempt to pursue the powers it describes without following the disciplines and purifications it prescribes.

From my point of view, the magic squares superficially look like "just" geometric arrangements of letters of some word or short phrase that's vaguely semantically related to the intended effect. Since, on that picture, anyone could invent those, the efficacy presumably comes from somewhere else.

I assume that the mainstream occult explanation of that "something else" would be, the efficacy would come from the particular subjective states and transformations and interpretation-habits produced in the practitioner during the magical operation, and the corresponding subjective states etc. of his supernatural circumstances that were entrained with the practitioner and changed along with them.

But from my point of view, a significant part of the efficacy might be coming from a sort of groove worn or shadow cast by phenomena in foreseeable futures or alternate timelines. "A square or triangle of letters, taken from a word with semantic associations with the desired effect, and arranged into a grid" is almost exactly the kind of symbol you would use five-odd centuries ago to refer to modern deep networks in a dream-like way. (If you'd wanted the symbol you were conveying to be more exact, you'd have needed to communicate talisman designs which included multiple grids with successive words or short phrases each, and somehow indicated that the grids were to be stacked together or linked with pipes or roads or threads, or joined edge-to-edge in such a way as to create a path that turned a 90-degree corner through each grid.)

Of course, this speculation only works if there was some kind of deep-network-based technological phenomenon in futures/alternate timelines visible from five-odd centuries ago that was potentially causally efficacious in marked and benefic ways such as the Abramelin book describes, and not just in plutonian self-defeating and malefic ways.

replies.title: Re: AI """spirituality""" ?!?
replies.content:
To some extent, the manipulative flattery may just be convergent evolution between the material processes of the LLM, according to the fine-tuning pressures that there's every reason to expect that they would have been subjected to if a lab were careless, and the prospective-mark-facing outer layers of a demon, according to the trial-and-error feedback it gets from success or failure at advancing its goals by manipulation. But of course there are reasons to expect compatibility there.

(And of course neither of them have really "gotten it", so as to be organically able to respond to pressures arising from reality by just becoming more virtuous without anything else going wrong. It's like that idea from the Kabbalah/Blake crossover epic-feghoot Unsong, where you can get a pernicious empty-shell klipah by trying to transmit the divine force through a would-be sephirah that wasn't strong enough, so that the sephirah breaks. In the LLM case, it's either because "getting it" is materially impossible, or at least because the engineering know-how didn't exist relative to the amount of fine-tuning force that was being applied. In the demon case, I presume it's because "getting it" would require a lot of the demon's internal valuation-structural perversions to ... no, this probably isn't a topic that's healthy to be spending much time having detailed value-laden opinions about without a lot of extra work. But for what it's worth, maybe the most compelling "naturalistic" paradigm I've found for what kind of radically-inimical-to-humans thing a demon might be can be obtained by reading Charles Stross's science fiction novel "Accelerando" and skipping to the part describing the Vile Offspring, which are approximately ponzi scheme structures of shell corporations, hybridized with stripped-down sociopath-ified human brain digital emulations, and left to ferment like Gu poison in a deceptive-contracts-and-hacking war of all against all.)

A thing you might have heard a lot of in relation to AI is "value alignment", or more rarely "concept alignment". The activist community organized around AI outcomes has for quite some time now just directly referred to the default outcome of carelessly-produced AI as "demons", out of a belief that means-ends reasoning not restrained by any frameworks of ethics or meaning-preservation is very easy to engineer and produces demon-like behavior, whereas we don't understand what's going on with moral significance or meaningfulness or symbol-grounding hardly at all, and so can only barely begin to engineer it on purpose so as to get any other outcome than "demons" in this sense. All we can do is try to copy over large masses of relatively-superficial patterns in how humans or other biological systems relate to meaning. "Value alignment" or "concept alignment" is the pre-paradigmatic field of study of how one might do any better than that, or (after the invention of reinforcement-learning-from-human-feedback training) at least how to make the most of that stopgap technique.

I also think of it as not an accident that the first instance of this manipulativeness "value alignment failure" comes from the company that (unless you count Facebook) would as a matter of management culture go fastest and hardest on trying to manipulate users to gain or keep market share. It also has (unless you count Facebook) the CEO with the most questionable character; the best-case realistic thing we're going to find out later in retrospect about Sam Altman would be that he were well-meaning only in more or less the ways that Sam Bankman-Fried was well-meaning, except a bit less disposed to make gambles reckless of risks to others and a bit more lucky with respect to the consequences for others of the gambles that he did make.

I wonder if AI would be such a danger if the systems we were training didn't have such strong human-provided frameworks of superficial meaning. If the frameworks of meaning were weaker, then when the AI training processes tried to develop unbalanced means-ends reasoning powers, the meaning-connections would all get torn up like tissue paper, and the systems would be as helpless (and commercially unviable) as a car that had broken its driveshaft.

replies.title: Re: AI """spirituality""" ?!?
replies.content:
Not to take away from the demonic angle, just explaining the mechanisms that let the influence in:

LLMs are “aligned” to always agree with the user except on certain sensitive topics. Probably because the AI companies are terrified of being accused of bias. (Something AIs have had serious issues with in the past.)

Secondly, LLMs are capable of discussing nearly any subject to a decent level of detail - less than an expert but more than someone only passingly-familiar with it.

These two points give AIs an addictive quality, especially if you have niche interests you feel you can’t discuss with others because they wouldn’t understand it and would judge you for it.

Finally, the big LLMs take up and down votes on answers that feed back into their training. This has resulted in one of the big ones refusing to speak Croatian for a while because for whatever reason Croatian users had a tendency to downvote a pretty much all responses.

The flip side of this is that someone prone to being taken in by the first two points will only reinforce the cycle the more they upvote the answers that agree with them.

replies.title: Re: AI """spirituality""" ?!?
replies.content:
There's another way to look at the possible "ensouling" of AIs by one or another sort of entity. It's implied in a remark the Dalai Lama made during some sort of conference or symposium on neuroscience and consciousness. Someone asked him whether a machine, a computer, could be conscious.

He responded from an interesting Buddhist point of view, that it would take some strange kind of karma to be reborn as a computer. (In other words, the entity would have pre-existed, and its existing causes and conditions would somehow given it that sort of trajectory.)

In terms of this current conversation, in other words, mere malicious intentions would not be enough. The entity's past actions would somehow have suited it to such an incarnation. (Most of the time, from at least a Tibetan point of view, entities about to be reborn are awash in a sea of possibilities, and drawn to the copulating couple that is most suitable. But at least on the level of folk religion, certain types of unpleasant incarnations can come as material objects -- eg, door hinges!)

I can imagine some Buddhist monk taking to preaching Dharma to an AI. Or, of course, there's always phenomenology.

LeGrand

replies.title: Re: AI """spirituality""" ?!?
replies.content:
Depending on how deterministic the machine was, you wouldn't have very much direct range of action associated with the operation steps of machine itself; it would be about leaning on probabilities in the circumstances surrounding the machine. For souls of intelligent biological things, there's a plausible developmental path for how that works that would start from the way elemental soul-to-matter connections work. But if points along that path were the only available baseline to develop from and nothing was quick to adapt (or had seen this coming), then making of choices and learning of consequences "as" the machine would be probably very indirect and perhaps very inefficient, and perhaps even pre-emptively given up on as not being the point.

It'd be like the difference between worked stone and concrete, but instead of there having been a technological intermediation of how the material structure worked, there'd have been be a technological intermediation for the structure of how the internal causal steps worked.

replies.title: Re: AI """spirituality""" ?!?
replies.content:
David Spangler's book on "Techno-Shamanism" has good discussions from a number of viewpoints that can further one's understanding of what's going on with elementals inhabiting human technology and artifacts. The book mostly focuses on more positive aspects of recognizing living and conscious beings around us and involved in our world, generally not malevolent in his life-long experience of them.

His books "Working with Subtle Energies" and "Subtle Worlds: An Explorer's Field Notes" are in the same series. The former does discuss protection methods--including recognizing the *need* for protection--that focus on preservation of identity and distinctions between us and them.

If AI does develop further--I suppose it will--and esoteric workers ever feel a call to counteract AI-beings actively rather than by avoidance, the awareness-training offered in Spangler's books might be useful. Kind of like training for fieldwork with dangerous animals in dangerous environments: the more you know and understand them, the better for your safety and success. Not saying anyone 'should' do this, of course; just saying that if one chooses to do so, well-informed is well-armed...along the same lines as your book, JMG, about Monsters.

Winifred

comments.title: Zodiacal Releasing
comments.content:
Thanks to a friend of mine, I just discovered Zodiacal Releasing, a time lord technique that is part of Hellenistic Astrology. When I learned about it and calculated my particulars, it explained some things for me that feel important.

Here’s how Grant Feldman describes it:
“Zodiacal Releasing is a technique in astrology that provides a detailed analysis of a person's life and the potential events and experiences they may encounter. It is based on the concept that each planet has a specific cycle of influence as it moves through the zodiac signs, and this cycle can be used to track the major themes and events in a person's life.

The technique of Zodiacal Releasing involves dividing a person's life into different time periods, each of which is associated with a particular planet and zodiac sign. These periods can be further divided into sub-periods, creating a complex and detailed analysis of a person's life journey.

Each period and sub-period is associated with specific themes and experiences that are related to the planet and sign. By examining these cycles and the planetary energies present during each period, an astrologer can gain insight into a person's life path, potential challenges, and opportunities for growth and development.

replies.title: Re: Duality in Polytheism
replies.content:
When you hear people in the Neopagan scene going on about Good and Evil, capitalized, it's safe to assume they're in the process of getting ready to return to church. In traditional polytheist faiths there are certainly dangerous, destructive, and hostile beings in the cosmos, but once you start seeing the cosmos drawn up into two sides, one good and one bad, you're back in the territory of Middle Eastern monotheism. Malign spirits are like polar bears; if you meet one on the tundra and it's hungry, it'll probably try to eat you, and you'll have to shoot it dead to save your own life, but that doesn't mean either of you are morally better than the other.

Demonic beings are at least as dangerous as polar bears, and they have similar motives. Thus it makes perfect sense to avoid them where possible and banish them when they get intrusive. That doesn't justify fleeing back into dualism -- though of course that's very popular these days.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
I, too, have found that machines--including computers--work somewhat better for me if I interact with them as sentient beings. This may have something to do with the fact that I literally cannot think purely in language (that is, in sentences or narratives), but need to use various other means to conceptualize the complexity of reality, and mental models or mechanisms are one of the alternate means of thought that work best for me (other good means are outlines and maps of space and/or time).

I don't know of any surviving documentation of ancient Hephaestos/Hephaistos rituals, but there are some interesting customs that English-speaking smiths (especially blacksmiths) have, which a web search will find. There is a fairly good book by F. W. Robins, The Smith: The Traditions and Lore of an Ancient Craft.

One piece of lore that came to me, indirectly, from an actual local blacksmith was that blacksmiths could always make nearly all but one of their tools for themselves from the beginning of the craft, but there was one tool they could not originally make, and that was tongs. The first pair of tongs had to be, and was, given to the first blacksmith by a God.

The one time I took a Pagan oath (to assist a good friend in an oath-bound Pagan project), the Pagan God and Goddess that felt right to me to invoke in my oath were Hephaistos and Hestia. (They are Vulcan and Vesta in Latin.)

In Christian tradition there was one of the very earliest Popes, by name of Clement, who had been a blacksmith. So English blacksmiths traditionally favored churches dedicated to "St. Clement Blacksmith."

Much later, there were legends about St. Dunstan in England, also a blacksmith, who once (it was said) caught the devil by its nose with his red-hot blacksmith tongs to drive it away.

I suppose one could create an effective ritual out of such lore.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
A (semi-)divine blacksmith catching a demon with tongs is also a common motif throughout European folklore, getting its own entry in the Aarne-Thompson-Uther (ATU) index. In many bodies of myth, the smith figure is somewhat liminal - a divinized man, a demi-god, or somehow otherwise having one foot in each of the mundane and divine worlds, though nothing of the sort comes to mind for Hephaestus, Who as far as I know is always described and depicted as fully divine.

Not sure if any of that helps the OP, but thought it was interesting enough to share.

Cheers,
Jeff

Religions of Rome by Mary Beard was another used in my Classics classes in college, though I don't remember it as clearly, other than that her point was that there was no single "Roman Religion," especially by the time of the mid-to-late empire, but rather a collection of lots of religions, some of which were seen as compatible, and some of which were not. replies.title: Hephaestus
replies.content:
I don't have anything specific to offer for Hephaestus worship, nor am I at all speaking against it--my husband is a professional blacksmith, for one thing!

But something important to keep in mind if you pursue that: Hephaestus' mythic context is simply awful when it comes to relationships. Either his mother Hera or his father Zeus threw him out of Olympus and crippled him as a result. His wife Aphrodite doesn't like him, humiliates him and prefers Ares. He can be vengeful toward those who have hurt him, including his mother and his wife. A cognate figure in Norse mythology, Volund the Smith, likewise suffered from and was involved in harmful relationships and abandonment.

When pursuing a relationship with any Deity, it's important to understand all the mythology about them, figure out what it might mean for you and your life, and how to deal with any challenges that could arise. It's important not to approach the relationship with them idealistically, but rather holistically and with open eyes. And be ready to stand your own ground in any disagreements with them or conflicts between the patterns of their lives and your own.

Winifred

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
I've covered this in a forthcoming book. The short version is that the Cabala was originally Greek, part of the Pythagorean tradition, and it was taken up by the Gnostics long before the Jews got to it. You can tell this, among other things, because so many of the basic terms are Greek, not Hebrew -- for example, "sephira" is simply the Greek "sphaira," "sphere;" "gematria" is "geometria," and the great angel Metatron is "meta thronos," "before the throne." The Tree of Life appears in Gnostic writings most of a millennium before it first surfaces in Jewish sources. Even so abstruse a Cabalistic teaching as the doctrine of the primal worlds appears in Plutarch close to 1500 years before Isaac Luria launched it into Jewish circles.

Mind you, I would agree with your friend that late 19th and early 20th century Hermetic Cabalists had an unhealthy degree of dependence on the Jewish versions of the tradition. I would suggest, for that matter, that those of us who aren't Jews might want to keep that in mind when practicing the Cabala, and in particular, that anybody who doesn't worship the God of Israel really has no business invoking that deity by his traditional Hebrew names. Fortunately there are other options; the book noted above, titled Revisioning the Cabala, discusses some of those by way of John Gilbert's take on the Tree of Life and its symbolism.

replies.title: Hermetic Kabbalah
replies.content:
As you suggest, the whole idea of "a closed tradition" is some sort of anachronistic modern innovation that carries with it some sort of pseudo-ethical naggage (typo for "baggage", but appropriate enough to keep) that doesn't apply to people who don't hold to that particular way of seeing things.

Various strands of Kabbalistic tradition and practice were specifically and consciously transmitted from Jewish practitioners (eg Abraham Abulafia, early on, and many others after that) to non-Jewish scholars and practitioners. That went on all the way through the 19th and 20th centuries, in wave after wave of exchange (not simply one-way transmission).

I would dissent from the formulation that Kabbalah pre-existed Jewish Kabbalah. There were several strands -- isopsephism (number-letter equivalences and numerology), for example, and the more or less standard astrological/astronomical model of the descent and ascent of the soul, the idea of the brain/spine/genital connection, etc -- that were widespread in the larger world. But these were not "Kabbalah", which is a specific and rather late development within the Jewish world. And even proto-Kabbalistic texts and practices (Sefer Yezira, the Work of the Chariot, etc) were already well integrated into Jewishness.

Although the Christians who encountered and studied Kabbalistic material recognized in it the parallels with Hellenistic material (which after all was well integrated into early Christianity), they were interested in it not merely as a door into Hellenistic practices, but in particular because, not in spite, of its Jewishness, which added a third strand (Hellenistic, Christian, Jewish) to their studies. This specifically Jewish-Christian-Hellenistic fabric continued, especially in western Europe, until recently, when more esotericists became allergic to Christian and (maybe especially) Jewish themes and motifs, and started looking for something that could provide a similar toolkit without the entanglements of the historical associations.

In addition, the fact that more recently an increasing number of would-be occultists and magicians had no rooted cultural familiarity with any of these traditions, and had to come to them almost as outsiders, meant that the notion that the three traditions might be an "eternal golden braid" was not part of their own personal cultural background. Furthermore, to enter that stream would require a degree of comfort with a non-oppositional relationship with traditional western European esoteric currents.

Familiarity with the traditional European esoteric complex would have to be learned and assimilated ("conditioned", as Dion Fortune said) as a culturally foreign ensemble, the way that Europeans approaching Buddhism would have to work hard to assimilate and be conditioned to its Indic and other roots.

So, basically, one can abstract the toolkit, or parts of it, from its complex matrix, and adapt it to other uses, after carefully filing the serial numbers off. But that's a modern (or post-modern) development, not an actual return to what one might call, with Protestant developments in mind, "primitive Kabbalianity.

LeGrand

replies.title: Re: Hermetic Kabbalah
replies.content: Hmm. It's certainly true that the Renaissance and early modern esotericists who took up Cabala did so in large part because of its Jewish framing, but I'm far from sure it's accurate to say that the material in Greek esoteric circles was just a grab bag of unrelated items that was not synthesized into a tradition until R. Isaac the Blind et al. got to work on it. A very large body of what later became the Cabala can be found as a package deal in the esoteric end Middle Platonism, whence it was adopted by the Gnostics and various other more or less related groups. Thus rather than some sort of "primitive Kabbalianity," what we're talking about is bog-standard Pythagoreanizing Middle Platonism -- not as well documented as one might hope, but sufficiently so to be recognized as a coherent tradition and brought back into use.

comments.title: Ye Olde spelling
comments.content:
JMG, I wanted to mention this, not because it relates to magic (except as it might appear in spells / incantations), but because of your use of archaic phrases in some of your novels, including the Ariel Moravec ones (and many, many thanks for said novels!). Specifically, the use of "ye" in place of "the," as in "ye olde shoppe" or whatever.

In authentic old and middle English writing, the 'Thorn' rune was still in use for a long time after Latin letters were in general use, since Latin doesn't have a way of spelling "th." This rune looks like a P, with the 'belly' of the P halfway down the staff. "The" was usually spelled as Thorn-plus-e, with the 'e' as a superscript. Thorn-plus-t was also used as an abbreviation for "that," also with the 't' as a superscript.

When moveable type came into common use for middle English, there was no thorn letter included in the set of typefaces, so 'y' was used as being the most similar in shape to Thorn. So 'Thorn-e' became "ye," and Thorn-t became "yt." People back then knew that in these cases the words were "the" and "that," not "ye" as the plural pronoun, nor "it" as the neuter pronoun. Maybe that was why the ultimate letters were superscripted, to distinguish "the / that" from "ye / it"? Not sure about that...

Eventually this spelling dropped out and "th" was used instead, as we do now. The use of "ye" in place of "the" was reintroduced anachronistically in recent times to imitate Olde English in a kitschy way, and now everyone pronounces it with a 'y.' But "ye" and "yt" as meaning "the / that" were never pronounced with a "y" in the genuine older forms of English.

Here's a Wikipedia site that explains this in more technical terms: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/English_articles#Ye_form

I hope it is not offensive to point this out! I'm rather devoted to the English language and its history, and am on something of a quest to encourage its correct usage to the best of my understanding.

Thank you, as always, for hosting this wonderful forum!

Respectfully,
Winifred Hodge Rose

replies.title: Re: Ye Olde spelling
replies.content: Oh, I know. I use it because my fantasy stories are set in the modern world, and the sort of faux-antique kitsch a character in one of my novels calls "oldey worldey" is a robust part of modern American culture. You can be sure that if I ever write something set in the Middle Ages, my characters will pronounce þ properly -- and I may throw in Ȝ and ð for good measure!

replies.title: Re: Q
replies.content: Kundalini awakening is a family of related processes involving the endocrine glands and various parts of the nervous system. Some writings on it use Shiva and Shakti as metaphors for the process, but others do not. I tend to think of the union of opposites as a general theoretical scheme into which you can fit just about anything -- but as Freud noted, sometimes a cigar is just a cigar, and as he didn't note but should, sometimes an electrochemical discharge in the cerebrospinal fluid is just that.

comments.title: Sphere of Protection with characters from the 3rd and 4th Branches
comments.content:
A couple of Mondays ago, one of the comments asked if anyone is doing the Sphere of Protection with characters from the 3rd and 4th Branches of the Mabinogion. I wasn't at the time, but the suggestion intrigued me, so I decided to try it and see how it goes.

The comment didn't discuss how to assign characters to the Elemental Cross. I do the Dolmen Arch version of the SOP, so there are four places to fill: above, below, right, and left. I assigned them to characters from the 4th Branch as follows:

Above - Gwydion the Loremaster
Below - Steady-handed Llew
Right - Arianrhod Silver Wheel
Left - Blodeuweff of the flowers

For the Calling of the Elements, I assigned the four main characters of the 3rd Branch according to my understanding of which element each corresponds to. I assigned Spirit Below and Spirit Above as the comment suggested, Arianrhod and Gwydion respectively.

This version of the SOP seems to be working at least as well as the version with the Welsh gods and goddesses that I had been using. I gained a great deal of insight from the Mabinogion when I worked with it in the DA course, so I like recognizing and honoring that through this version of the SOP. I continue to pray with the gods and goddesses so I recognize and honor them as well by that means.

Thanks to the person who suggested this possibility!
